title: AI Readiness Quiz
description: Test your understanding of AI readiness assessment, data auditing, process mapping, skills analysis, and building a business case.

questions.question: In the 5-Dimension Readiness Framework, which dimension carries the most weight?

answers.text: Budget Alignment (30%)
answers.feedback: Not quite. Data Maturity carries the highest weight at 30%. AI runs on data, so the quality and accessibility of your business data is the single biggest factor in your AI readiness.

answers.text: Team Capability (30%)
answers.feedback: Not quite. Data Maturity carries the highest weight at 30%. AI runs on data, so the quality and accessibility of your business data is the single biggest factor in your AI readiness.

answers.text: Data Maturity (30%)
answers.feedback: That's right. Data Maturity carries 30% of the readiness score because AI runs on data — if your data isn't in good shape, AI tools won't have much to work with.

answers.text: Process Documentation (30%)
answers.feedback: Not quite. Data Maturity carries the highest weight at 30%. AI runs on data, so the quality and accessibility of your business data is the single biggest factor in your AI readiness.

questions.question: You run a small landscaping business. Your customer details are in a notebook, your quotes are in Word documents on your laptop, and your invoicing is in Xero. Which data quality issue should you address first?

answers.text: Upgrade your internet speed
answers.feedback: The biggest issue here is customer data locked in a paper notebook. It's not accessible, searchable, or usable by AI. Digitising that information should come first.

answers.text: Move your customer details from the notebook into a digital system
answers.feedback: Spot on. Customer data trapped in a notebook isn't accessible, searchable, or usable by any AI tool. Moving it into a digital system (even a simple spreadsheet or CRM) is the highest-impact first step.

answers.text: Switch from Xero to a different accounting platform
answers.feedback: The biggest issue here is customer data locked in a paper notebook. It's not accessible, searchable, or usable by AI. Digitising that information should come first.

answers.text: Start collecting social media analytics
answers.feedback: The biggest issue here is customer data locked in a paper notebook. It's not accessible, searchable, or usable by AI. Digitising that information should come first.

questions.question: Which of the following are qualities of good data for AI purposes? (Select all that apply)

answers.text: Accurate — the data is correct and up to date
answers.feedback: That's right. The four qualities of good data are accurate, complete, consistent, and accessible. Data quality matters far more than data quantity.

answers.text: Voluminous — you have as much data as possible
answers.feedback: The four qualities are Accurate, Complete, Consistent, and Accessible. Volume (having lots of data) isn't one of them — data quality matters much more than quantity for most small business AI use cases.

answers.text: Complete — there are no significant gaps
answers.feedback: That's right. The four qualities of good data are accurate, complete, consistent, and accessible. Data quality matters far more than data quantity.

answers.text: Consistent — the same information is recorded the same way
answers.feedback: That's right. The four qualities of good data are accurate, complete, consistent, and accessible. Data quality matters far more than data quantity.

answers.text: Accessible — you can actually get to it when needed
answers.feedback: That's right. The four qualities of good data are accurate, complete, consistent, and accessible. Data quality matters far more than data quantity.

questions.question: You've mapped your processes and found that responding to customer enquiries takes 10 minutes each and happens about 5 times per day. What's the approximate monthly time cost of this task?

answers.text: About 5 hours per month
answers.feedback: Let's do the maths: 10 minutes x 5 per day = 50 minutes/day. Over about 22 working days per month, that's approximately 18 hours. The closest answer is 16-17 hours. This shows why process mapping matters — small tasks add up.

answers.text: About 10 hours per month
answers.feedback: Let's do the maths: 10 minutes x 5 per day = 50 minutes/day. Over about 22 working days per month, that's approximately 18 hours. The closest answer is 16-17 hours. This shows why process mapping matters — small tasks add up.

answers.text: About 16-17 hours per month
answers.feedback: Right. 10 minutes x 5 times/day = 50 minutes/day. Over roughly 22 working days, that's about 18 hours per month — a significant chunk of time that AI could help reduce.

answers.text: About 25 hours per month
answers.feedback: Let's do the maths: 10 minutes x 5 per day = 50 minutes/day. Over about 22 working days per month, that's approximately 18 hours. The closest answer is 16-17 hours. This shows why process mapping matters — small tasks add up.

questions.question: According to the Impact/Effort matrix, which types of AI opportunities should you tackle first?

answers.text: Strategic Projects — high impact, high effort
answers.feedback: Quick Wins (high impact, low effort) should always come first. They deliver noticeable results with minimal investment, which builds confidence and evidence for tackling bigger projects later.

answers.text: Quick Wins — high impact, low effort
answers.feedback: Exactly. Quick Wins give you the biggest return for the least effort. They build confidence and generate evidence for your business case before you tackle bigger projects.

answers.text: Self-Service — low impact, low effort
answers.feedback: Quick Wins (high impact, low effort) should always come first. They deliver noticeable results with minimal investment, which builds confidence and evidence for tackling bigger projects later.

answers.text: Deferred — low impact, high effort
answers.feedback: Quick Wins (high impact, low effort) should always come first. They deliver noticeable results with minimal investment, which builds confidence and evidence for tackling bigger projects later.

questions.question: You're planning AI training for your small team of 6 people. Which approaches align with the 'Community of Multipliers' strategy? (Select all that apply)

answers.text: Send everyone to a full-day AI workshop at the same time
answers.feedback: The Community of Multipliers approach involves identifying 1-2 champions, investing in their training, and having them share knowledge through show-and-tells. It doesn't involve training everyone simultaneously or mandating AI use.

answers.text: Identify 1-2 digitally confident team members as AI champions
answers.feedback: That's the approach. Train a few enthusiastic people well, let them experiment, then have them share their knowledge with the rest of the team through regular show-and-tells.

answers.text: Invest in proper training for your champions first
answers.feedback: That's the approach. Train a few enthusiastic people well, let them experiment, then have them share their knowledge with the rest of the team through regular show-and-tells.

answers.text: Have champions run regular show-and-tells to share what they've learned
answers.feedback: That's the approach. Train a few enthusiastic people well, let them experiment, then have them share their knowledge with the rest of the team through regular show-and-tells.

answers.text: Mandate that all staff must use AI tools immediately
answers.feedback: The Community of Multipliers approach involves identifying 1-2 champions, investing in their training, and having them share knowledge through show-and-tells. It doesn't involve training everyone simultaneously or mandating AI use.

questions.question: What is the average ROI that NZ businesses report from their AI investments?

answers.text: 1.5x — $1.50 back for every $1 invested
answers.feedback: The average NZ AI ROI is 3.7x — $3.50 returned for every $1 invested. Combined with the fact that 75% of businesses started for under $5,000, the financial case for AI is strong.

answers.text: 2.0x — $2 back for every $1 invested
answers.feedback: The average NZ AI ROI is 3.7x — $3.50 returned for every $1 invested. Combined with the fact that 75% of businesses started for under $5,000, the financial case for AI is strong.

answers.text: 3.7x — $3.50 back for every $1 invested
answers.feedback: Correct. NZ businesses report an average AI ROI of 3.7x, meaning $3.50 returned for every $1 invested. And 75% of businesses got started for under $5,000.

answers.text: 10x — $10 back for every $1 invested
answers.feedback: The average NZ AI ROI is 3.7x — $3.50 returned for every $1 invested. Combined with the fact that 75% of businesses started for under $5,000, the financial case for AI is strong.

questions.question: You scored 45/100 on the readiness framework and want to apply for government AI funding. Which programme specifically co-funds AI adoption for small businesses?

answers.text: The R&D Tax Incentive only
answers.feedback: The AI Advisory Pilot Programme is the specific programme designed for SMEs. It co-funds up to 50% of eligible costs, capped at $15,000 per business, and is delivered through the Regional Business Partner Network.

answers.text: The AI Advisory Pilot Programme — co-funding up to $15,000
answers.feedback: That's right. The AI Advisory Pilot Programme offers co-funding up to 50% of eligible costs, capped at $15,000 per business — $2,500 for an AI roadmap plus $12,500 for implementation. It's delivered through the Regional Business Partner Network.

answers.text: There is no government funding available for AI adoption
answers.feedback: The AI Advisory Pilot Programme is the specific programme designed for SMEs. It co-funds up to 50% of eligible costs, capped at $15,000 per business, and is delivered through the Regional Business Partner Network.

answers.text: The Callaghan Innovation AI Grant — up to $100,000
answers.feedback: The AI Advisory Pilot Programme is the specific programme designed for SMEs. It co-funds up to 50% of eligible costs, capped at $15,000 per business, and is delivered through the Regional Business Partner Network.
